question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Golimumab and Upadacitinib dual therapy medication for arthritis and ulcerative colitis are currently available on prescription on the NHS.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. Where NICE has recommended the use of golimumab (Simponi®) and upadacitinib (Rinvoq®) in the treatment of arthritis and ulcerative colitis, these medicines are available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines.Currently, golimumab (Simponi®) and upadacitinib (Rinvoq®) are licensed for a number of conditions including rheumatoid arthritis and ulcerative colitis, either as monotherapy or in combination with methotrexate. Use of both treatments in combination is not included within the terms of the marketing authorisation for either product and as such use for these purposes is considered to be ‘off-label’ and therefore not routinely available in HSC.However, the individual funding request (IFR) process provides a mechanism to consider requests from clinical consultants for treatments for individual patients that fall outside the range of services routinely commissioned. Clinical decisions such as these are a matter for the patient’s specialist consultant, in discussion with the patient or carer. Further information can be found online at Individual Funding Requests (IFRs) - HSC (hscni.net).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the permanent resolution to GP indemnity cover will be ring-fenced funding separate to GP practice funding.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland.  A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model of provision to a fully state-backed indemnity scheme. I have given a commitment for this work on options to be completed by the end of this calendar year, to allow for substantive engagement with GP representatives on the topic as part of negotiations for the 2025/26 GMS contract.  Funding for the scheme will form part of this work and therefore I am unable to confirm future funding arrangements at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for further detail on his plans to introduce a new lung screening programme.
answer_text: The former Minister of Health, Minister Swann, accepted the recommendation by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme for asymptomatic people with a history of smoking, aged between 55-74 years old, in tandem with the enhanced integration of smoking cessation services.We are at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be.  We will be working with colleagues across Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK to establish the resources required and the likely timeline. This will be a very significant service development encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services. This work will require input and collaboration from multiple partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to his statement to the Assembly on 10 December 2024, how his plan to invest £15 million will be used in (i) GP services and (ii) community pharmacy.
answer_text: The Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through a Commissioning Plan, developed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland. Work will be commencing in early 2025 to update the current commissioning plan and outline the service priorities for the next three years, aligned with the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan. The additional £15m, if secured, is intended to support the delivery of new community pharmacy services once the commissioning priorities are agreed through the refresh of the Commissioning Plan.My Department is also committed to supporting the role of pharmacists in general practice and to support them to contribute fully to optimising the health outcomes of our population as the clinical leads for medicines within the general practice team. “GPP NI 2030: A strategy for general practice pharmacy in Northern Ireland” sets out a vision for developing the general practice pharmacy workforce, including the introduction of new advanced practice pharmacist roles. Further development of the GP Pharmacy workforce through advanced practice roles will be included in the Multidisciplinary Teams model, once that is rolled out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to change the criteria of the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme.
answer_text: My Department’s Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS) remains open to new applications. The grant scheme covers 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs.  You will be aware that I instructed my officials to review the process following the severe flooding in Autumn 2023. I am pleased to confirm that following this review the eligibility criteria for the scheme has been reduced and the application process is now significantly shorter, making it easier for the homeowner to apply. This has taken into account the need for a more considerate approach for homeowners and to address the significant emotional and financial distress they face following a flood event. For example, properties which are likely to benefit from a government flood alleviation solution in the medium term will not automatically be precluded from the scheme. Furthermore, homeowners will only have to provide evidence of only one flooding event in the past 25 years, as opposed to the original criteria of two events in 25 years.Whilst the financial contributions as part of the scheme remain the same, it is anticipated that the changes to the eligibility criteria will reduce the application process by up to 6 weeks. These changes are effective now and my officials will be updating the website in due course. Property level protection is a very important piece of my Department’s Toolkit to manage the impact of flooding to prevent or reduce water damage, thereby minimising the financial loss and protecting valuable possessions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the communication that orthopaedic patients can expect to receive in relation to (i) when they will have their treatment; and (ii) the support available to them, while they wait for surgery.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts actively communicate with patients who have been waiting longer than 52 weeks, primarily through text messaging, to ensure they feel supported, understand they have not been forgotten, and are reassured they will be contacted as soon as their treatment is ready to proceed. As part of this communication process, Trusts are also conducting regular waiting list validations to confirm whether patients still need to remain on a waiting list for assessment or treatment.Trusts also provide a range of services to support people on waiting lists while they wait for treatment.  This includes information and advice, signposting to additional sources of internal and external support, and access to specific services as appropriate, such as diet and exercise programmes, smoking cessation advice, mental health and wellbeing services and peer support etc.  The updated Elective Care Framework (ECF), published in June 2024, recognises the need to support patients who are on waiting lists to better self-manage the condition they have been referred for, as well as their wider health and well-being needs. It sets out the need for development of a policy in this area, which will aim to ensure patients on waiting lists have the best possible outcomes and, through empowerment and support, ensure that their health is optimised and that they are as prepared and as well as possible for their treatment.  My Department is in the early stages of work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to having a single referral to treatment measure, to track the patient journey for those on waiting lists.
answer_text: My Department fully supports the development of a referral to treatment target. This target will require patients to be tracked from when they are referred from their GP and onwards through all subsequent assessment diagnostic and treatment episodes. Such tracking will require a clear and succinct set of rules and well-defined definitions from referral to treatment. From a technical perspective, when the new regional encompass system is fully rolled out next year, Northern Ireland would be well placed to be able to measure the full patient journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the information and support that a patient can expect to receive should their surgery or healthcare appointment be cancelled during the winter period.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts endeavour every day to prioritise patients who are planned to have surgery and attend outpatient appointments.Surgery and outpatient appointments are not routinely cancelled during the winter period, as the Health and Social Care Trusts aim to manage both unscheduled and scheduled care.Every effort is made to ensure hospital outpatient cancellations are kept to a minimum.  However, should it be necessary for an outpatient appointment or Inpatient/Day Case admission to be cancelled, the patient would be notified by telephone and/or text message, followed by letter confirming the hospital cancellation.  Where possible, the letter will also include the detail of an alternative offer of a further appointment.The Department’s Winter Preparedness Plan has been published.  As in previous years, the Plan sets out a series of initiatives and priorities for tackling the extra pressures during the winter period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the provision available to people with long-term health conditions to help them manage their mental health, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care TrustBelfast Health and Social Care Trust’s (BHSCT) Mental Health Services will accept referrals from General Practice when the GP deems the person is suffering a mental ill health as a consequence of a long-term health condition.There is significant evidence that having a physical health condition has an impact on your psychological wellbeing and mental health, and that in turn this can impact on the outcomes of your physical recovery and treatments.BHSCT has within the Psychological Services Division, a Clinical Health Psychology Service, directly working within physical health services, offering psychological support and interventions to those who experience psychological distress as a consequence of living with/ experiencing both an acute physical health challenge (e.g. an acute orthopaedic trauma) or a long-term physical health condition.  BHSCT provide input both on the wards and outpatient clinics. Referrals are accepted from the Physical Health teams involved in the patients care. Referrals are not accepted from GPs. These services have often been commissioned to work directly with a certain physical health presentation including for example Diabetes, Cystic Fibrosis, Renal Problems, Pain Conditions, Cancer, Burns, Intestinal failure presentations.BHSCT also has what is known as a Generic Clinical Health Psychology service which was historically available within the Royal Victoria Hospital and offers broader support across inpatient wards. On the Trust merging it also  extend to other hospital sites. This is not a commissioned service and is a small resource.Northern Health and Social Care TrustThe Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) is commissioned to provide a dedicated psychology service provision to adults (18 years and over) living with long-term physical health conditions via Clinical Health Psychology Services and the Stroke Psychology Service.  The Clinical Health Psychology Service provides psychological interventions including provision of psychological therapies, contribution to interdisciplinary self-management programmes, consultation/care planning and training to health care professionals.  The Service currently provides this across the following specialisms: cancer, cardiology, diabetes, chronic pain, renal and respiratory via primary care and secondary care referral pathways.  There is also a long-Covid service provision in place until 24/25 financial year-end. The Stroke Psychology Service provides time-limited psychological interventions, including psychological therapies, for adults who are in the early months of recovery. Cognitive assessments may also inform these interventions. Where appropriate, there may be later onward referral to non-statutory services offering relevant emotional support or counselling. Further Stroke Psychology Service provision includes training for stroke colleagues and consultation / case discussion in both stroke and non-stroke services.  NHSCT is als

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures that will be put in place to protect orthopaedic surgery from cancellations during the expected winter pressures, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All Trusts plan to maintain their elective orthopaedic work during winter and minimise the number of elective cancellations. Trusts will continue to review their trauma demand on a daily basis and where there is an increase in trauma demand the clinical team will consider different options to manage this demand. Actions may include managing the patient at home until an operation date can be confirmed or cancelling planned elective procedures. Any decision to cancel elective activity will be determined by the level of clinical risk and the associated subspecialty.Earlier this year a bid was made by my Department for £135m to support elective services and more recently a further £10m bid was submitted as part of October Monitoring. Unfortunately, these bids were not supported, which was a missed opportunity to improve our elective services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has given any further consideration to increasing the amount of financial support available through the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance for domestic properties that have been flooded.
answer_text: I have not given any further consideration to increasing the amount financial support payable under the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance.  Any changes to the amount will require additional funding and approval by the NI Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children on the waiting list for spinal fusion surgery; and (ii) the length of time on the waiting list, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: On 9th November 2023, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust launched ‘Encompass’ – a new electronic patient record. The system also went live in Belfast HSC Trust on 6th June 2024 and Northern HSC Trust on 7th November and its rollout across the other Trusts will continue in 2025. Consequently, as the Belfast Trust continues to transition to completely digitised health records, data is currently unavailable on the number of children on the waiting list for spinal fusion surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the progress of the approval of Omaveloxolone for patients with a diagnosis of Friedreich’s ataxia; and (ii) whether Omaveloxolone will be made available for use through the NHS.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.NICE are currently in the early stages of evaluating omaveloxolone (Skyclarys®) for the treatment of Friedreich’s ataxia in people 16 years and over, with final guidance expected to publish in August 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.However, the individual funding request (IFR) process provides a mechanism to consider requests from clinical consultants for treatments for individual patients that fall outside the range of services routinely commissioned. Clinical decisions such as these are a matter for the patient’s specialist consultant, in discussion with the patient or carer. Further information can be found online at Individual Funding Requests (IFRs) - HSC (hscni.net).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the introduction of Enhertu for breast cancer patients.
answer_text: The Department of Health has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland and where found to be applicable they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations.On 29th July 2024, NICE published final guidance in which Enhertu® is not recommended for the treatment of HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer in adults. NICE have been unable to recommend Enhertu® for treating HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer within the NHS as the most likely cost-effectiveness estimates based on the price offered by the manufacturer are above the upper end of the range NICE considers an acceptable use of NHS resources.The Department of Health formally endorsed the NICE guidance as applicable in Northern Ireland on 20th August 2024 and so Enhertu is not routinely available for the treatment of HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer in the HSC.However, the individual funding request (IFR) process provides a mechanism to consider requests from clinical consultants for treatments for individual patients that fall outside the range of services routinely commissioned. Clinical decisions such as these are a matter for the patient’s specialist consultant, in discussion with the patient or carer. Further information can be found online at https://online.hscni.net/our-work/ifrs/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is undertaking to raise awareness about epilepsy and the impact it can have on those living with the condition.
answer_text: While there is no active programme by the Department to raise awareness, Epilepsy services are available across the Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland. Each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust in Northern Ireland provides specific services to support individuals with a diagnosis of epilepsy. These services include specialist nursing support, access to neurologists, and various community resources. Clinicians and Epilepsy Nurse Specialists will provide specific information and support to patients to help them manage their condition.My Department also values the exceptional work of the third sector and specifically that of Epilepsy Action Northern Ireland. My Department provides core grant funding to Epilepsy Action Northern Ireland to support it in its work which includes awareness and building understanding of the condition across sectors through initiatives such as online epilepsy awareness training and educational resources for healthcare professionals, school staff, and employers.  My Department is also taking forward a Regional Review of Neurology Services. The voice of people with neurological conditions, including epilepsy, has been central to the Review’s analysis of current challenges within neurology services and to the identification of a programme of recommendations for improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is considering reviewing the age requirement for prostate-specific antigen testing.
answer_text: Screening for prostate cancer is currently not recommended by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC). They last considered the evidence for screening for prostate cancer in 2020 and concluded that screening was not recommended.  This is because of the poor ability of the prostate specific antigen (PSA) test to predict cancer, which could lead to unnecessary tests that carry risks of potentially life-changing harm. Additionally, some prostate cancers may not produce elevated PSA levels, leading to false-negative results that provide false reassurance. Prostate cancer is rare in men under 50 but the risk increases with age. The UK NSC has commissioned a prostate cancer screening evidence review and cost effectiveness model, and it is expected to review this work during 2025. The UK NSC process will include a public consultation. The UK Health Departments, through the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR), have invested in a significant programme of research to address the evidence gaps in this important area. A clinical trial (called the TRANSFORM trial), which will compare the most promising tests and provide evidence about the best way to screen for prostate cancer, is due to start in early 2025.My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the services available to support people with a diagnosis of epilepsy in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust provides specific services to support individuals with a diagnosis of epilepsy. While the services available varies across each Trust, in general services include specialist nursing support, access to neurologists providing detailed assessments, diagnostic evaluations, medication management and treatment plans, access to mental health services, community support programs and disability support as required.In addition, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is the Regional Neurology Centre for Northern Ireland providing highly specialised clinics on a regional (Northern Ireland-wide) basis.  My Department is also taking forward a Regional Review of Neurology Services to improve services for patients with neurological conditions including epilepsy. The review, is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years, determining the associated investment required and outlining an implementation plan. As part of the review, workstreams were established to consider key parts of the Neurology pathway including Outpatient referral and long-term support. The Regional Review of Neurology Services completed its final report in October 2024 and it is anticipated, will drive improvements across Neurology services, including access to Outpatient assessment and diagnosis. It is estimated that the consultation will commence early next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any actions he has taken to address inequalities in women's cardiovascular health.
answer_text: Last week I met with the British Heart Foundation prior to the launch of their Bias and Biology Northern Ireland report regarding the Heart Attack Gender Gap. I welcome the report which highlights issues of under-awareness, under-diagnosis and under-treatment of cardiovascular disease among women, and provides a number of helpful policy recommendations which my Department will now consider. I recognise the need for improved education and information on a wide range women’s health issues and risk factors, to support women and healthcare professionals in the identification, management and treatment of conditions including cardiovascular disease, which is one of the leading causes of death among women. This will form part of the Women’s Health Action Plan which my Department is developing. One of the guiding principles of the Northern Ireland Cardiac Network is to work to ensure equity of access to high quality cardiac care for all of the population. My Department will continue to work closely with the Cardiac Network to consider the report and any improvements that could be applied to current diagnostic and treatment pathways. While there have been many advances in cardiovascular care, these have not benefitted all people equally. There is a clear association between socio-economic, environmental and lifestyle factors and cardiovascular outcomes in Northern Ireland. I am committed to tackling these health inequalities through a range of interventions, including: My Live Better initiative. which I recently launched in two pilot locations, which aims to better align existing resources and services with need. Legislation on Minimum Unit Pricing for alcohol in Northern Ireland, which I plan to bring forward in the Assembly as soon as possible. The Tobacco and Vapes Bill which I intend will extend to Northern Ireland with the support of Assembly colleagues. The development of a new obesity strategy and a regional obesity management service which my Department is currently progressing, subject to resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the recent Invest NI trade missions.
To ask the Minister of Health whether the translation service used by Health and Social Care Trusts provides an option to translate for those who speak Irish.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has commissioned contractors to provide written translation and transcription services to the NI Public Sector, including Health and Social Care Trusts, through qualified and experienced translators.  This written translation contract has three providers and covers a wide range of languages including Irish.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on his Department’s latest annual review of workforce planning; (ii) whether there are plans to increase employment opportunities for newly-qualified Occupational Therapists; and (iii) to detail the Health and Social Care Trusts they will be located in.
answer_text: (i) Working with key stakeholders, my Department identifies future long-term workforce need through a rolling programme of regional strategic workforce reviews.  As set out in the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People and its Second Action Plan (2022-23 to 2024-25), the Department is currently developing a new strategic workforce planning review programme.  This will have an increased focus on programmes of care aligned to service transformation and will address issues such as staffing numbers and skills mix across all healthcare professional groups.(ii) and (iii) Registered occupational therapists are a key element of the health and social care workforce.  Over recent years my Department has made progress in growing this element of the HSC workforce, which has increased by 22.7% (227.6 whole time equivalent staff) over the period June 2018 to June 2024.The regional recruitment of newly qualified occupational therapists is led by the Business Services Organisation and takes place once each year.  The availability and location of posts during any one recruitment exercise is based on current vacancies and anticipated staff turnover.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the business case proposal for nocturnal dialysis at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: SPPG are not in receipt of a business case from Southern Trust for nocturnal dialysis.  SPPG fully supports the principle of re-shaping service models and resources to better meet the needs of patients and welcomes engagements with providers to explore this potential.SPPG is aware of a multi-centre Randomised Clinical Trial study ‘Nightlife’ taking place across the UK to consider the benefits of nocturnal dialysis in comparison to daytime treatment.  This will conclude in December 2024 with findings available mid-2025 and is expected to provide formal evidence across a range of outcome measures as to the comparative benefits of day and nighttime dialysis.  This will be of considerable help in informing discussions on future service delivery models for haemodialysis including the potential for nocturnal services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to address medicine shortages as a result of Brexit impacting people with epilepsy or Parkinson's disease.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly complex, regulated and global industry, therefore medicines shortages are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicines supply chains to the UK, and works closely with the other devolved administrations, including my Department, to put in tried and tested national and local mitigation measures if supply disruptions do arise. Through UK-wide and local medicines management shortages groups, my Department is notified of medicines supply disruptions and the actions which can be taken to mitigate the impacts on patients in Northern Ireland (NI). My Department is not currently aware of medicines shortages occurring as a consequence of EU Exit for patients with epilepsy or Parkinson’s disease, however, there have been supply disruptions to various strengths and preparations of epilepsy treatments for other reasons over the past year. Whilst most of these supply disruptions have now been resolved, there continues to be some preparations experiencing supply disruptions.Through these medicines management shortage groups, agreed guidance has been issued to healthcare professionals providing advice and actions to take via Medicines Shortage Notifications. Additionally, healthcare professionals can utilise the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service’s medicine supply tool, which is updated regularly by DHSC and provides up-to-date information on medicines shortages.Carbamazepine 100mg/5ml oral suspension, which is licensed for epilepsy, was added to the list of medicines that cannot be exported from the UK or hoarded by wholesalers on 22 May 2024, and currently remains on this list. Other treatments can be added or removed based on availability of medicines.If required and deemed appropriate, other tools and processes can also be enabled including further communications to healthcare professionals such as National Patient Safety Alerts, Serious Shortage Protocols and the Northern Ireland MHRA Authorised Route (NIMAR).My Department will continue to work with DHSC and the other devolved administrations to ensure mitigations are put in place to ensure patients in NI can continue to access appropriate treatments if supply disruptions do arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the engagement between his Department and the Patient Client Council’s engagement panel in relation to co-production of the Serious Adverse Incident review process.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of staffing in Daisy Hill maternity unit (i) prior to; and (ii) during the divert that was implemented on 21 September until 23 September 2024.
answer_text: The level of staffing in Daisy Hill maternity unit in the week leading up to the divert ranged from 76%-84% of normal staffing levels.The level of staffing in Daisy Hill maternity unit during the divert from 21st to 23rd September was deemed critical with a low of 42%.  The upper range was 90% of normal staffing levels which was only for 12 hours within the 48-hour period.Staffing is considered critical if under 57% on the Daisy Hill Site for maternity services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering any measures to invest in General Practice pharmacy to ensure these medical professionals are valued and the service resourced so that sufficient career opportunities and progression are available.
answer_text: In 2015 the Department of Health made a five-year commitment to invest in a General Practice Pharmacists (GPP) service, introducing new roles for pharmacists to work alongside GPs. Full implementation of the service was achieved by 2020.Every GP practice in Northern Ireland now has a pharmacist as an integral part of their clinical team, working alongside GPs and other healthcare professionals to improve the safety and quality of prescribing and improve patient outcomes. 303.5 wte posts have been funded, with a recurrent annual cost of £19.5m. Evaluation of the service has demonstrated that GPPs represent excellent value for money for the HSC, and the potential for GPPs to further contribute to the transformation of Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland is clear. The launch of “GPP NI 2030 - A Strategy for General Practice Pharmacy in Northern Ireland” realised a commitment made by my Department in our Pharmacy Workforce Review, published in 2020, to set a strategic direction for the future development of the general practice pharmacy workforce. GPP NI 2030 sets out how GPPs will contribute to optimising the health outcomes of our population as the clinical leads for medicines within the general practice team. It also sets out an ambition to develop and expand the skill mix of the workforce to include pharmacy technicians and introduce new advanced pharmacist practitioner roles to benefit patient care.Realising the full potential of the GPP workforce will require additional investment to stabilise and develop service capacity and capability. Full implementation of the recommendations set out in “GPP NI 2030” is subject to confirmation of funding being available and will also require prioritisation, workforce mapping and planning to ensure realistic implementation. It is not possible to fund full implementation from within the Department’s existing resources and so delivery is dependent on securing the additional resources required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the minimum number of staff required to meet safe staffing levels in a maternity unit.
answer_text: Safe staffing levels in a maternity unit are determined by a number of factors including the  type of unit (i.e. Alongside Midwifery Led Unit, Consultant Led Obstetric unit), the size of population served, the number of admissions and the complexity of mothers admitted.  Not only would this vary across units, but it would also vary at different time of the day depending on activity levels.  There are multiple specialities involved in maintaining safe and effective maternity services including Obstetrics,  Midwifery, Neonatology, Anaesthetics, Medical Specialities, Laboratory, Radiology and Support Services.   To make the best use of their resources and maintain a safe service, across many specialities health Trusts will quite often move staff across units and may also initiate diverts between hospitals, during times of high demand /acuity or to manage workforce challenges to maintain the safety of mothers and babies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Department’s latest annual review of workforce planning, including any plans under consideration to increase employment opportunities for newly-qualified nurses.
answer_text: Working with key stakeholders, my Department identifies future long-term workforce need through a rolling programme of regional strategic workforce reviews.  As set out in the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People and its Second Action Plan (2022-23 to 2024-25), the Department is currently developing a new strategic workforce planning review programme.  This will have an increased focus on programmes of care aligned to service transformation and will address issues such as numbers and skills mix across all healthcare professional groups. Registered nurses and midwives are a key element of the health and social care workforce.  Over recent years my Department has made significant progress in growing this element of the HSC workforce, which has increased by 18.1% (2710 whole time equivalent) over the period June 2018 to June 2024.The regional recruitment of newly qualified nurses is led by HSC Trusts and takes place twice each year.  The availability of posts during any one recruitment exercise is based on current vacancies and anticipated staff turnover, which fluctuates substantially.  The most recent regional recruitment exercise for newly qualified nurses received more than 700 applications and all successful candidates have been offered posts.  It should be noted that some of these applicants have chosen to remain on waiting lists as they seek preferred hours, location and/or areas of work. In addition to recruitment to HSC Trusts, posts for newly qualified nurses are also available across a range of settings in Northern Ireland’s wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role played by General Practice Pharmacists in the health and social care system.
answer_text: In 2015 the Department of Health made a five-year commitment to invest in a General Practice Pharmacists (GPP) service, introducing new roles for pharmacists to work alongside GPs. Full implementation of the service was achieved by 2020, and today every GP practice in Northern Ireland has a pharmacist working as an integral part of their clinical team.Evaluation of this service has demonstrated that GPPs provide value for money for the HSC through supporting the delivery of prescribing efficiencies, as well as other vital improvements in the safety, efficiency, effectiveness and consistency of prescribing within GP practices. Pharmacists also release much needed capacity within the general practice team so that GPs can focus more of their time on the care and management of patients with complex medical needs.Informed by this evaluation, my Department recently published “GPP NI 2030 – A strategy for General Practice in Northern Ireland” which outlines plans for the future development of the general practice pharmacy sector. This delivers a commitment in the Pharmacy Workforce Review 2020 to set a strategic direction for the future development of the general practice pharmacy workforce. GPP NI 2030 sets out how GPPs will contribute to optimising the health outcomes of our population as the clinical leads for medicines within the general practice team. It also sets out an ambition to develop and expand the skill mix of the workforce to include pharmacy technicians and introduce new advanced pharmacist practitioner roles to benefit patient care.My Department recognises that realising this vision will require collaborative working and new approaches to the planning and delivery of general practice pharmacy services, and has established governance arrangements to oversee the consistent delivery of high-quality services and to progress implementation of the recommendations outlined.Realising the full potential of the GPP workforce will require additional investment to stabilise and develop service capacity and capability. Full implementation of the recommendations set out in “GPP NI 2030” is subject to confirmation of funding being available and will also require prioritisation, workforce mapping and planning to ensure realistic implementation. It is not possible to fund full implementation from within the Department’s existing resources and so delivery is dependent on securing the additional resources required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the annual spend on locum (i) GPs; and (ii) consultants in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on locum staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ locum staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years there have been concerns of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognition that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department therefore has prioritised significant work to alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes urgently addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure. Work continues regionally across HSC, under the Agency Reduction Implementation Group (ARIG) with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. The Nursing and Social Work staff groups focussed on by ARIG has shown a reduction of approximately £40m from 2022/23 to 2023/24.Alongside this, work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, in addition to a dedicated workstream examining the reform of the Bank system. Further work will immediately focus on reducing ‘Off-Framework’ agency for Medical and Dental, and other staff groups.The annual spend on locum (i) GPs; and (ii) consultants in each of the last 5 years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, is detailed in tables 1-4, outlined below:Table 1 - Total expenditure by Trust for Trust Employed locum GPs for 5 years, 2019/20-2023/24  2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24 £'000£'000£'000£'000£'000Belfast HSC Trust4,6215,7205,4455,7826,060Northern HSC Trust22231067712South Eastern HSC Trust2,7353,1952,8742,7613,371Southern HSC Trust183265364199295Western HSC Trust213415309368Total7,7749,6189,0988,8559,746Table 2 - Total expenditure by SPPG for GP Practice Locum for 5 years, 2019/20-2023/241Area2019/20£'0002020/21£'0002021/22£'0002022/23£'0002023/24£'000Belfast452354498462526Northern644655430617611South Eastern314371296286567Southern4254214413841,286Western4233044338612,056Total2,2582,1052,0982,6105,046It should be noted the costs included in the table above are based on actual claims submitted in respect of each year and exclude Employer’s costs paid by SPPG.Increases to local expenditure in the Western, Southern and South Eastern areas have been made with a view to maintaining service provision in light of GP contract handbacks.Table 3 - Total Expenditure by Trust for Directly Employed Locum Consultant for 5 years, 2019/20-2023/24 2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24 £'000£'000£'000£'000£'000Belfast HSC Trust1,7272,6041,615338646Northern HSC Trust4453417276771,070South Eastern HSC Trust1,44

answer_text: The Equality Impact Assessment, which contains the Human Rights Impact was published in draft form alongside the Public Health Bill consultation on 5 July 2024. The impact assessments will be reviewed as part of the consultation response review process and published alongside the summary report of the consultation. The impact assessments can be found on the Departmental website at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/consultation-new-public-health-bill-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on waiting lists for children awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery; and (ii) to detail any plans his Department has in place to reduce current waiting times, particularly for vulnerable children and young people.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  My officials within SPPG have now carried out some initial scoping on the question that you have raised with senior officials in BHSCT and have been informed of the following.There are 93 children currently on the waiting list. Each case is constantly assessed by the specialist team at the RBHSC and dealt with as quickly as possible based on clinical priority and chronological order. Due to the specialist nature of this procedure it is only carried out at the RBHSC.  A Paediatric Surgeon in BHSCT is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable for potential transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  BHSCT are carrying out discussions with Alder Hey to ascertain the feasibility of this arrangement.  If agreed clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be put in place to determine what children will be transferred.Belfast Trust is planning to provide a waiting list initiative in October on a Friday, with patients being nursed in RBHSC over the weekend. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  Belfast Trust is also in discussion with South Eastern Trust to determine if Gastrostomy surgery could be provided in the Ulster Hospital by Belfast consultants.  Belfast Gastrostomy Nurses will provide training to staff in SET to help facilitate this. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  There is also currently a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery.The Trust are also examining the potential of partnership working with Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  In the meantime, paediatricians and paediatric surgeons along with other clinical teams at the RBHSC will continue to support these children and their families.My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the publication of the Review of South East Flooding Autumn 2023, what measures will be put in place in relation to Newry and South Armagh.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the salary paid to foundation training year pharmacy graduates in (i) hospital and Health and Social Care Trust settings; and (ii) community pharmacy settings.
answer_text: There is currently a 2-employer model for Pharmacy Foundation Training Year (FTY) students in NI. Presently, students completing their FTY in the community pharmacy setting are provided a Departmental funded training grant, which is paid directly to pharmacy contractors via the Business Services Organisation (BSO). Payment of grants by the Department to community pharmacy contractors who host FTY students, has been a long-established arrangement in NI. The grant is not a stipulated salary.The FTY grant has been increased for the community sector over the last few years to bridge the gap between it and the hospital sector, and was again increased earlier this year in April, to give a total of £27,511 per trainee for a 12-month placement. This includes £22,011 for the student and meets the 2024 National Living Wage (NLW) allowance for 21 years old and older (£11.44/hr, 37 hours, 52 weeks), plus a tutor allocation of £5,500. Employers may supplement the grant if they wish.There is no grant for hospital FTYs. Students who choose to complete their FTY in the hospital sector, enter a formal contract of employment with the respective HSC Trust, where they receive a salary paid to them by the Trust and which is set in accordance with the NHS Terms and Conditions of Service (Agenda for Change) national pay system for NHS staff. Pharmacy hospital FTYs enter at the lowest point on the Band 5 pay scale, currently £28,407 per annum for a 12-month placement.An Initial Education and Training (IET) Short Life Working Group (SLWG) has been overseeing implementation of new standards that are reforming the education of pharmacists in NI, including reform of the FTY programme. The SLWG comprises stakeholders from all pharmacy sectors and organisations who have a role in delivering the IET programme and the work of this group informs Departmental policy decisions. The matter of FTY pay was discussed and considered by the SLWG during preparations for the new IET programme, commencing in August 2025. Different options were considered and the preferred direction to reform the FTY, is to move away from the current 2-employer arrangement to a Single Lead Employer (SLE) model. This would provide a standard grant to all students in all sectors.  In advance of the SLE model being implemented, the IET SLWG considered the options available for the 2025 intake and advised that whilst their preferred option is to move to pay parity, a split salary model would be retained for this year. This was to make optimal use of current available funding and to support the maximum number of students (which is circa 200 across both universities in NI). The intention is to move towards pay parity for the 2026/27 cohort of students, however this is subject to securing the necessary uplift in funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the delay for children with a diagnosis of cancer to access clinical trials.
answer_text: All children with a cancer diagnosis should be advised of clinical trials that are available to them. I am aware that there has been a reduction in clinical trials that are available in Northern Ireland in recent years. There are many reasons for this, including clinical capacity to fulfil the requirements associated with running a clinical trial.Officials within my Department are currently working with the HSC to understand what can be done to increase access to clinical trials. Work is also underway to develop a new Cancer Research Strategy for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why his Department provides a lower level of funding for the foundation year training grant in community pharmacy compared to that of hospitals and Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the implementation of the Autism Strategy 2023-2028; and (ii) to detail any plans his Department has to improve autism services for adults in the immediate term.
answer_text: (i) The Autism Strategy 2023-2028 was published on 13 December 23 with a two year Delivery Plan for 2023-2025 which will be reviewed on an annual basis. The Delivery Plan sets out the actions to be achieved against commitments, timeframes associated outcomes and the delivery partners involved.   Work on implementation of the strategy is underway across government departments and public bodies and progress will be assessed through the annual monitoring report. As a significant volume of information has been received from delivery partners, officials are currently analysing and summarising this information.  Once collated and approved, the report will be shared with colleagues in the Assembly and published on the Department’s website.  Going forward monitoring and reporting on the implementation of the Strategy will form part of the role of the Independent Autism Reviewer.(ii) A Review of Autism provision across the region has been undertaken.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism provision - recruitment of this post is ongoing and it is hoped that an appointment will be made shortly. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his engagement with the junior doctors committee in relation to the ongoing pay dispute.
answer_text: I acknowledge a welcome breakthrough in talks between the UK Government and the BMA Junior Doctors’ Committee in England. The BMA’s junior doctors committee (JDC) in England accepted the Government’s pay offer on 16 September 2024. The offer is worth 22.3% on average over two years. Work will now be undertaken to confirm the financial implications for Northern Ireland. This will help inform my Department’s engagement with the NI junior doctors’ committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has had any engagement with Newry Early Years Children and Family Centre to find an alternative premises for their services post-September 2024.
answer_text: Newry Sure Start is one of the 38 projects delivering my Department’s Sure Start programme; the project had been delivering its services from Newry Early Years Children and Family Centre. My Department has been engaged with the Department of Health (administrator of the Sure Start programme) and with Early Years (Lead body for Newry Sure Start) on related premises matters.  Newry Sure Start has now relocated and continues delivery of services from alternative Newry premises.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the delivery plans in relation to the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework will commence.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the expected legislative changes arising from the sentencing Bill.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide three cycles of funded IVF treatment.
answer_text: I fully support the New Decade New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF treatment for eligible women. The recent expansion to one full cycle is to be welcomed, but I recognise that there will be those that were disappointed that we were unable to offer further cycles at that time. Further expansion of the service will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Working Time (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2023, how his Department is calculating pay and holiday claims for staff who regularly work over their contractual hours.
answer_text: Both my Department and HSC Employers are aware of the issues and would like to see a speedy resolution to ensure all staff are paid at the correct level during periods of leave. A working group has been established to address the ongoing position, with representatives of my Department, HSC Employers, and relevant trades unions (TUS). Unfortunately, due to TUS concerns in respect of ongoing legal cases, progress on this matter has been slower than would be desired. Resolution of the issues must be by agreement between employers and TUS, each of whom are committed to coming to an agreed position reflecting the importance all sides place on identifying the correct solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the relocation of pre-op services at Daisy Hill Hospital, to detail (i) why staff were not transferred to Daisy Hill Hospital to support the service; and (ii) whether the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has commenced recruitment to fill the vacancies for the service at Daisy Hill Hospital.
answer_text: There has been no relocation of pre-operative services at Daisy Hill Hospital. The presence of pre-operative assessment staff on a hospital site is not incumbent to the achieving of pre-operative services. Staff within the pre-operative services across SHSCT undertake pre-operative tasks pertaining to patients requiring assessment for surgery across all of the SHSCT hospitals.The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has commenced recruitment to fill the vacancies for the service at Daisy Hill Hospital, and subject to availability of staffing and recruitment timelines, would hope to resolve this in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on closing the Non-domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: Question 4 - AQO 643/22-27It is frustrating that the future of RHI is still undecided. It is a problem I inherited, and a problem that should have been dealt with long ago. A permanent solution, as raised in last year’s Court of Appeal judgement, is necessary for all legitimate participants on the Scheme, and for the taxpayer. Delay risks further litigation and the urgent need to close the scheme is underlined by Ofgem withdrawing their administrative services by April 2026 at the latest.I first circulated an Executive Paper on 4th April 2024.  It seeks to uplift tariffs and confirm the Executive’s intention to close the scheme. This confirmation is needed if my officials are to develop a detailed proposal for closure, and to negotiate with Treasury on using the funding available for another scheme. It is frustrating that the Executive has not been allowed to make a decision on the future of RHI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planned flood alleviation measures for (i) Newry city centre; and (ii) South Armagh.
answer_text: My Department are currently progressing a study to further identify flood risk in Newry and assess the viability of potential flood alleviation measures to address this. It is expected that an overall preferred option will be identified in Summer 2024. The project team are also currently engaging with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council with a view to potentially accelerating a section of the proposed Flood Alleviation works through the Council’s Theatre and Conference Facility project.As part of the Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP), my Department’s projects are prioritised and brought forward depending on their position. South Armagh has not been identified as a Significant Flood Risk Area (SFRA) and therefore we do not have any plans for significant works in the area

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the (i) planning application; (ii) delivery timeline; and (iii) funding secured for the new children’s hospital.
answer_text: The New Children’s Hospital and demolition of Bostock House planning application was submitted on 21st August 2018 and approved on 18th April 2019. This approval is not subject to expiry as the approved works have commenced.  The New Children’s Hospital is projected to commence on site in early 2025 subject to Full Business Case approval in summer 2024. The building works are programmed for a period of 4.5 years to complete in summer 2029. A commissioning phase will follow with the hospital expected to be operational by Q4 2029/30. As the New Children’s Hospital is an Executive flagship project, it will be prioritised and funded from the Department’s capital budget through to completion.  Whilst it is not possible to identify and secure revenue funding so far in advance of the completion of the project, it is expected that revenue funding to support the running and additional staffing of the New Childrens Hospital will be prioritised from future budget settlements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s review of staff ratios for childcare settings.
answer_text: My officials have prepared a consultation paper which sets out a number of options in relation to staff:child ratios and the requirement for GP-countersigned health assessments to be provided by individuals seeking to work in childcare.  The consultation will commence when the current period of purdah has concluded.  This will be the first stage of a wider review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when free parking permits will be in place for hospital staff; (ii) whether all staff will be provided with free parking permits; and (iii) whether there will be particular provision for emergency or on-call staff.
answer_text: As a fair recognition of the hard work and dedication of HSC staff, all staff parking permits became free of charge from 12 May 2024.  There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with a parking permit.Trusts have a range of supports in place to help encourage a reduction in the demand for onsite staff car parking including free off-site staff parking spaces with free staff shuttle buses to and from work, cycle to work and car share schemes, cycle shelters and showering facilities onsite to encourage cycling, running or walking to work, free evening and weekend parking  passes for parking out of hours, as well as the provision of bespoke Personal Travel Plans for staff. To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a free on-site parking permit, the Department has requested that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The assessment criteria takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport.Staff parking permits are not automatically allocated to emergency or on-call staff, however on-site parking will be provided when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the publication of the Review of Neurology Services report; (ii) for his assessment of the May 2024 summary report from the Migraine Trust in relation to the standard of migraine care; and (ii) to detail his Department's plans to improve migraine care.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services is progressing well. My Officials have recently completed a programme of pre-engagement with key stakeholders to inform the final report of the Review and feedback from that engagement is currently being considered. It is anticipated that the Final Report of the Neurology Review will be completed in the coming months.I recognise the challenges within headache and migraine services highlighted by the Migraine Trust Report ‘The impact of Migraine on People in Northern Ireland and Opportunities to Improve Care’. The findings of this Report have been considered within the context of the recommendations arising from my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services. The Neurology Review has identified a range of recommendations which will improve services for all neurology patients, including those with headache and migraine. These include the introduction of referral management services to assist with the triage of outpatient referrals; and the provision of timely advice and guidance to GPs to assist with the effective management of neurology patients in primary care where appropriate. In addition, consideration has also been given to the development of extended roles such as GPs with Enhanced Roles and Advanced Nurse Practitioners to support the management of people with neurological conditions including headache and migraine.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the flood compensation payments.

To ask the Minister of Health what consideration his Department has given to the introduction of (i) Wegovy; (ii) Ozempic; and (iii) Rybelus for addressing obesity and preventing obesity-related conditions.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In April 2023, the Department endorsed NICE Technology Appraisal TA875, which recommends semaglutide (Wegovy®) as an option for weight management, including weight loss and weight maintenance, alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults who meet the criteria outlined in the guidance. The recommendation from NICE is that Wegovy® must be prescribed in a specialist weight management service providing multidisciplinary management of overweight and obesity.  Northern Ireland does not currently have a specialist weight management service. My Department recently completed a public consultation exercise, seeking views on the potential establishment of a prototype Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS) in Northern Ireland. Such a service would provide a means for people living with obesity in NI to access treatment and specialist support which is not currently available to them. Analysis of the consultation responses has recently been completed, and decisions regarding the future development of this service will be subject to resource availability.In respect of Ozempic® and Rybelsus®, these are glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) products authorised for treatment of type 2 diabetes, however, are not licensed for the management of obesity and so use for this purpose is considered to be ‘off-label’.There have been ongoing UK wide and global supply disruptions to glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) products authorised for treatment of type 2 diabetes since mid-2023, including Ozempic® and Rybelsus® preparations. These supply disruptions have arisen due to an increase in global demand for these products for licensed indications as well as off-label use, including a significant increase in private supplies for weight loss purposes. Advice issued by the Department to healthcare professionals is clear that these products should only be prescribed for their licensed indications and should not routinely be prescribed for obesity on an off-label basis, in order to support maintenance of supplies for patients with type 2 diabetes.My Department expects all providers of healthcare services, whether Health and Social Care (HSC) or private, and all those with responsibility for prescribing to take appropriate account of national guidance such as Medicine Supply Notifications and National Patient Safety Alerts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people on the waiting list for their first outpatient rheumatology appointments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment are published quarterly, broken down by HSC Trust and specialty as well as weeks waiting.  These publications can be found at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the decision-making process for the fixed bridge design for the Southern Relief Road.
answer_text: Following an Executive Formation Act 2022 decision taken by the department, I undertook a review of the fixed bridge option.  My review considered a range of factors including engineering, economic, heritage impact and environmental assessments.  The additional cost to the project to include the provision of an opening bridge would be in the range of £25m - £30m. After carefully considering this information, I confirmed that the decision for a 50m span non-opening fixed bridge design for the Southern Relief Road remained unchanged.The publication of the draft Statutory Orders and EIAR for the NSRR scheme will provide an opportunity for anyone to comment on the current proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of early diagnoses of cancer that have been made since the opening of the Rapid Diagnosis Centres.
answer_text: Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs), opened in December 2022. The Vague Symptom Pathway for patients with vague but worrying symptoms that may indicate cancer has been rolled out incrementally to GP Federations across Northern Ireland since that date.Whilst it took some time for this service to be fully established, referrals are now being received province wide and RDC activity levels are steadily increasing.Up to 31 April 2024, a total of 105 people have been referred to red flag cancer pathways following a radiological diagnosis of cancer. This represents 16.6% of the total number of patients who have been seen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the provision of more special educational places for children aged 3-19 instead of short-term Specialist Provision in a Mainstream School.
answer_text: Specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPiMS) were previously known as Learning Support Centres (LSCs) and have formed an integral part of our education system and the continuum of special education provision for decades.The provision is underpinned by Article 3 The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, which sets out the primary ‘Duty to educate children with special educational needs in ordinary schools’, with clarification within the Code of Practice (1998) which states that 'the needs of most pupils will be met in mainstream schools and without a statutory assessment or a statement. Children with special educational needs, including those with statements, should, wherever appropriate and taking into account the wishes of their parents, be educated alongside their peers in mainstream schools’.  It is vital that there is appropriate places, across primary and post-primary provision, to support the growing number of children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) and to facilitate the development of clear pupil pathways within mainstream.My recent Statement to the Assembly (30 April 2024) indicated the biggest step change to capital planning and commitment to a far-reaching and long-term plan which will require sustained and increased capital investment of around half a billion pounds over the next decade.  This will create increased capacity for specialist education provision in both special schools and through SPiMS to ensure that every child and young person is happy, learning and succeeding in their education journey.Additionally, the department’s ongoing End to End (E2E) Review of SEN will inform the wider transformation of the whole system, ensuring that children with SEN have access to a high quality education and receive the right support delivered by the right people at the right time and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the engagement his Department has had with the Department of Education on the Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: Given my Department’s childcare responsibilities, I recognise the importance of partnership working with Education and a range of other Departments with an interest in the Childcare Strategy.My officials meet regularly with Education colleagues both informally, to exchange information relevant to our respective childcare roles and responsibilities, and through formal structures such as the All Party Group on Early Education and Childcare and the Cross-Departmental Task and Finish Group working on a Childcare Affordability Scheme for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to review the provision of resources such as music therapists, play therapists and sensory spaces, in order to meet the growing complexity of children's needs in special education.
answer_text: The provision of creative therapies to support children with special educational needs (SEN) is being considered within my department’s End to End (E2E) Review of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the outstanding works required at St Joseph's High School, Newry, for (i) toilet facilities; (ii) science laboratories; and (iii) a football pitch.
answer_text: I have asked officials in my Estate Operations Team to arrange a visit to St Joseph's High School to discuss the toilet facilities and science laboratories as my Department has no active schemes on the minor works programme.  However, I am aware of issues with damp in the walls of the science laboratories, which Education Authority (EA) Maintenance Service are currently investigating.  In early 2023, the EA Neighbourhood Team confirmed the availability of funding from Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to provide new pitches at St Joseph's High School for both school and community use.  Health and Safety (H&S) works to the slope and path leading to the grass pitches need to be completed before the pitch works can proceed.  It is currently anticipated that the feasibility report for these H&S works will be completed by the end of May.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding opportunities for sports clubs for the 2024-25 financial year for (i) programmes; and (ii) capital projects.
answer_text: You will be aware that Sport NI, my Department’s arm’s length body, disburses exchequer funding to the sports sector on behalf of the Department.This is an operational matter for Sport NI, and the Chief Executive has advised me as follows:“Sport NI is planning a new Creating Opportunities funding programme. The investment programme offers match-funding support from Sport NI through the Crowdfunder platform and will be open to organisations including Sports Clubs. Creating Opportunities is a programme designed to connect communities, enabling more people to take part in sport and physical activity. In particular, a focus of the investment is to create opportunities to ‘level the playing field’, so that accessibility and inclusion is felt and experienced by those who have been traditionally excluded from not only participating in sport but playing an active role within the club.In addition, Sport NI has plans to operate a further Community Planning Investment Programme for this year. Applications to this programme will come from District Councils, but as in FY23-24, it is anticipated that clubs (and other organisations such as Governing Bodies; Community organisations) could be involved as delivery partners for projects. Sport NI is still considering what capital programmes can be delivered this year and is resource dependent.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many unfunded and unstaffed beds, including beds in non-clinical spaces on wards, Emergency Departments and in corridors, were in use in each Health and Social Care Trust, during March 2024.
answer_text: Information on the number of unfunded and unstaffed beds, including beds in non-clinical spaces on wards, Emergency Departments and in corridors is not held centrally. Therefore, each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) was asked to provide a response.The Belfast and Southern HSCTs were unable to provide a response within the required timeframe. The responses received from Northern, South Eastern and Western HSCTs now follow.Northern HSCTNorthern HSCT responded that its systems do not record the required information.South Eastern HSCTSouth Eastern HSCT responded that across its acute wards on the Ulster, Downe and Lagan Valley hospital sites, there are a range of additional beds in place to assist in meeting the demands placed on its services. While the question mentions ‘unstaffed beds’ the Trust notes that, while these additional beds are unfunded, the Trust prioritises patient safety and these beds will have staff working within these wards who care for patients in these spaces. The table below provides an outline of the maximum number of additional beds which the Trust would have used each day during March, 2024. Dependant on demand, the Trust ensures that attempts are made to reduce these additional beds when possible. The additional beds play a key role in reducing congestion within its Emergency Department.Table 1. Maximum number of additional bedsused each day in March, 2024Ulster HospitalDowne HospitalLagan Valley HospitalAdditional beds in non-clinical spaces6987Unfunded beds9--Additional spaces within ED27--Source: South Eastern HSCT.In addition, across the Trust’s mental health wards (including Mental Health Services for Older People & Dementia) there were, on average, eight additional beds in place in March, 2024. These are unfunded and staffing was stretched to accommodate, requiring an increase in agency use.Western HSCTWestern HSCT responded that while bed usage fluctuates according to demand, in the month of March, 2024, the usage of unfunded beds in an Acute setting were 40 for Altnagelvin and 38 for South West Acute Hospital. It noted that while ward staff attend to the patients in the escalation beds, the Trust does not have funding for any additional staff to cover this increased workload.
