question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the availability of treatment for those living with phenylketonuria.
answer_text: The main treatment for Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a low-protein diet and controlled intake of certain foods. High protein foods should be completely avoided.Kuvan (also known as sapropterin) has been routinely available in Northern Ireland for treating hyperphenylalanaemia in people aged up to 21 years with PKU, and pregnant women, since October 2021 in line with the recommendations made in NICE Technology Appraisal (TA) 729.Subsequent to publication of NICE TA 729, the availability of sapropterin has been extended to eligible patients of all ages in England. This extension of use was based on a lower price being agreed by NHS England out with the NICE arrangements for access to a generic version of the medicine which is now available.In December 2023 my Department’s Permanent Secretary made a bespoke public interest decision under the Executive Formation Act to approve a regional position for the commissioning of generic sapropterin for patients of all ages. This decision was made on the basis that the acquisition cost of generic sapropterin available to the HSC is equivalent to the NHS England price and so treatment at this price is therefore deemed to be cost-effective by NICE.My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) issued a Service Notification to HSC Trusts on 2 February 2024 setting out the expectations for implementation of this decision, in line with Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for the requirement of a medical assessment for those working in the childcare sector.
answer_text: I am aware that this issue has been raised by group setting childcare providers as a potential barrier to recruitment. I have therefore asked my officials to consult on the vetting requirements currently in place for prospective childcare providers, along with provider:child ratios, as the first stage of the wider review of Minimum Standards due to commence in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his plans to provide interim support to the childcare sector ahead of the increase in the National Living Wage in April 2024.
answer_text: Minimum wage rates are a universal government intervention that apply to all employers across the United Kingdom.  While the rise in April 2024 will be good news for employees, it will undoubtedly increase the financial pressures on employers in several sectors, including childcare.  My officials, in conjunction with colleagues in other Departments are considering what short-term measures might be appropriate to ease the range of cost pressures which childcare settings and parents are facing.  My ability to provide any interim support will be dependent on the funds I have bid for to do so, being made available by the Executive in my 2024/25 budget allocation.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet providers myself, to hear from them directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of Multi-Disciplinary Teams.
answer_text: The MDT model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.Whilst the budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging. My Department remains committed to the further implementation of the MDT model across Northern Ireland,  and is developing a plan for roll-out over the next number of years subject to the availability of the necessary recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on flood support payments.
answer_text: On the 22nd February I announced details of the support package for businesses affected by recent flooding.  		The Enhanced Flood Support Scheme will open this week and will provide support of up to £100,000 to businesses that were directly flooded following the severe weather in late October and early November. 	The scheme will be delivered by district councils and will open for applications this week. The support is in addition to the £7,500 grants and rates relief that has been provided to date to affected businesses.		I also announced two hardship schemes to provide support for businesses and some other non-domestic premises that were impacted by the flooding but were not eligible for the previous £7,500 payment or the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme.  These schemes are expected to open within the next couple of weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide financial support for those householders who were impacted by the recent flooding.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide financial support to householders who were impacted by recent flooding.
answer_text: The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was introduced by the Executive in 2007. Its purpose is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding. It is not a compensation scheme and does not purport to be one; nor is it intended to replace or displace the assistance that households should receive from their insurers.		The Department for Communities’ Finance Support service is available for people who find themselves in a financial crisis, including those experiencing financial difficulty as a result of a disaster, e.g. fire, flooding, etc. The service provides financial help/support through the provision of Discretionary Support, Social Fund and Universal Credit Contingency Fund.		In addition, householders can contact the Department’s “Make the Call Service” to ensure they are getting all the money and supports they are entitled to.  Make The Call offers a telephony service and has a team of Community Outreach Officers who work from regional locations across Northern Ireland to conduct home visits and provide face to face assistance and support with benefit claim completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will increase the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance payment in line with inflation.
answer_text: The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was introduced by the Executive in 2007. Its purpose is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding. It is not a compensation scheme and does not purport to be one; nor is it intended to replace or displace the assistance that households should receive from their insurers.		Any amendments to the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance would require a paper to be tabled and approved by the NI Executive.		Currently there are no plans to review the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what progress has been made on a GP indemnity scheme.
answer_text: I am aware that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally and I am committed to identifying solutions to address this issue for Northern Ireland GPs.  Recognising this, my Department recently provided funding on a non-recurrent basis to support GPs with indemnity costs as part of a wider package to support General Practice this winter by boosting capacity.  My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland. A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model to state intervention that includes a number of state backed model options. Departmental officials are continuing to take forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place to develop a new Oral Health strategy.
answer_text: The current Northern Ireland Oral Health Strategy (OHS) was published in 2007 and, despite its age, the main oral health problems described in the document and the approaches to prevention advocated by it remain valid today.The challenges facing the DoH mean that, unfortunately, the significant resources required to develop a new wide-ranging OHS are not available at this time. However, the importance of analysing the oral health status of the population and recommending evidence-based approaches to health improvement is recognised.The Department of Health determined that the two sections of the Northern Ireland population that would benefit most from updated oral health improvement plans were children and older people.Unfortunately, the pandemic delayed the establishment of the two planned working groups, however significant progress was made and the Oral Health Improvement Plans for Children and for Older People are currently out for public consultation. The consultation will end on 4th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with the Children’s Hospice on the issues they have raised with a loss of funding; and for an update on Children’s Palliative care provision across the Health Service.
answer_text: (i) I refer the Member to the media statement that I issued on Friday in relation to this issue.  I recognise and applaud the vital work of the NICH and all hospices in Northern Ireland.As requested by me, DoH officials have engaged in detailed discussions last week with NI Hospice.It is clear to me that the financial pressures facing NICH go much deeper than any reduction of Departmental support.  This reduction involved the ending last summer of an additional but temporary annual payment of £170k. This decision was notified to the NICH in June 2023 and took effect from September 2023 which resulted in £85K reduction in this financial year.  I took the decision on Friday 09/02/2022 to reinstate that funding in full for this year.This payment is in addition to the core annual funding of £1.6m which my Department is providing directly to NICH this year. This core funding included a £420,000 component which had not been recurrent, meaning it would have to be subject to confirmation each year. I can confirm that, going forward, this will be made recurrent, giving the Hospice greater certainty.I am keenly aware that the financial challenges facing NICH go much deeper than this or next year. Like all health care providers, it has encountered significantly rising running costs, reflecting inflationary pressures.Regrettably, the NICH has advised my Department that the restoration of the full £170k for this year will not lead to the reversal of the service reduction made public this week.The NICH and NI Hospice parent body will need to develop longer-term solutions, to which I am sure Government will want to be supportive.Given the current absence of a budget for next year, and the well-documented financial uncertainties and challenges for Government, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.I am also very conscious that other hospice organisations, and many other health and care providers, are also grappling with growing budget pressures and will be seeking further statutory finance.I am grateful for the support and personal interest of the First Minister and deputy First Minister regarding NICH’s position. I am also aware of the fact that other NI Departments have provided financial support to NICH in the past.I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments. (ii) There is a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network (RPPCN) for NI that is chaired by a Paediatric Consultant and has key clinical membership from each Trust.  The NI Children’s Hospice are key participants on the Network.  Each Trust has a paediatric medical lead who coordinates and offers expert clinical advice on paediatric palliative care within that Trust.  A Regional Paediatric Pallia

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider reopening the cross-border reimbursement scheme to assist people on waiting lists for elective procedures.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish a workforce plan.
answer_text: Effective workforce planning is a key theme of the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People.  The aim is to develop and, by 2026, sustainably fund, an optimum workforce model for the reconfigured health and social care services.  This is not an individual Departmental Workforce Plan document, but rather an organic and responsive tool which brings together the workforce need identified through strategic workforce planning reviews.My Department oversees this regional rolling programme of long-term strategic workforce planning reviews.  It considers issues such as skill mix, career progression pathways and workforce design to support new models of care.  The aim is to secure workforce and skills supply over a 5 – 10 year horizon.  The optimum workforce model for the reconfigured health and social care services will incorporate the findings of all strategic workforce planning reviews led by my Department and will inform the Department’s annual commissioning of pre and post registration training places across the full range of healthcare professions.Day to day, operational workforce planning is also ongoing, carried out by employers such as HSC organisations and the independent, voluntary and private sector organisations from whom services are commissioned.  This type of workforce planning addresses issues such as service delivery, safe staffing levels, operational vacancy management and recruitment. The target date to develop and sustainably fund the optimum workforce model for the reconfigured health and social care services is 2026.  While I have no plans to publish a workforce plan, each strategic workforce planning review report accepted by my Department (which together constitute the optimum workforce model) will be published on the Department’s internet website.Strategic workforce planning reviews ongoing and planned within my Department at 8 February 2024 are set out in the following table: Strategic Workforce Planning Reviews at 8 February 2024Profession/Medical Specialty/Programme of CareOngoingCancer Services (Programme of Care)Diabetes Services (Programme of Care)HaematologyLearning DisabilityMicrobiology, Virology and Infectious DiseaseNeonatology and Paediatric Intensive CareObstetrics and GynaecologyOrthoticsPalliative CareParamedicsPathology ServicesPsychiatry and Mental Health: General Adult Psychiatry, Psychiatry of Old Age, Psychiatry of Learning Disability, Forensic Psychiatry, Child and Adolescent Psychiatry Psychotherapy, PsychotherapyPublic HealthSurgical specialties: General surgery (including transplant, breast and gastrointestinal surgery), Cardio-thoracic surgery, ENT surgery, Neurosurgery, Ophthalmology, Paediatric surgery, Plastic surgeryVascular SurgeryPlannedEmergency MedicineMedical specialties: General Medicine/Acute Medicine, Cardiology, Endocrinology, Gastroenterology, Geriatric Medicine, Genitourinary Medicine, Respiratory Medicine, RheumatologyStroke Services (Programme of Care)This

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many patients availed of the Cross Border Directive in (i) 2021-22; and (ii) 2022-23.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland (RoI) Reimbursement Scheme was put in place on a temporary basis for a period of 12 months from 1 July 2021 to help reduce Northern Ireland waiting lists and to provide an opportunity for some patients to have their treatment much earlier.  A further £5m was allocated to continue to operate the scheme beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), who administrate the temporary RoI cross border reimbursement scheme, do not record when patients have their treatment but rather when patients have been approved to claim reimbursement and when they submit claims.In 2021/22 the SPPG approved 1956 patients and in 2022/23 by mid-September a further 1711 were approved.  Of the total 3667 patients approved by September 2022, the SPPG has processed 1605 patient claims and received a further 494 claims for payment.  It is therefore estimated that approximately 2099 patients had benefitted from the reimbursement scheme in the period July 2021 – Sept 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact of the cost of living crisis on (i) the rate of domestic violence incidents; and (ii) the overall rate of crime.
answer_text: I very much recognise the continued impact of the cost-of-living crisis on families and communities across Northern Ireland.  The Department does not produce forecasts of overall crime or specific offences as there are a number of social and economic factors which cause an individual to engage in criminal activity.  I do however recognise that these increased socio-economic strains that are impacting our communities have the potential to increase the underlying causes of some offending behaviours.  Many of these issues impact across the remit of multiple Departments, and as such are not within the gift of the justice system alone to resolve.  My Department continues to work closely with partners through mechanisms such as the Community Safety Board, Support Hubs, and Reducing Offending Partnerships to ensure issues are addressed in a collaborative manner.I am also aware that living through a period when there are increased financial pressures as a result of higher energy, fuel and household costs can be difficult for anyone but for those suffering from domestic abuse the implications can be greater and their risk of abuse is increased.  It is important that those suffering from abuse, violence or a threat of violence in their home reach out and seek help.  Guidance for those who are affected by domestic abuse, and for whom the risk of abuse has increased due to the current cost of living impacts, is being prepared.  This will set out the services that are available to help and support those affected.  This guidance will be published on both the Department of Health and Department of Justice websites.  In addition, there will also be further bursts of the ‘Still Abuse’ awareness raising campaigns on domestic abuse, the domestic abuse offence and the support available through the 24hr Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what his Department is doing to provide support to businesses in the absence of a functioning Executive.
answer_text: As the lead for economic policy and strategy, my Department, working with its delivery partners such as Invest NI and InterTradeIreland, delivers a wide range of programmes and projects that support business development, investment and job creation in Northern Ireland. That entails a broad portfolio of business as usual activities aligned with our ambitious 10X Economic Vision including, for example, investment in skills, broadband and developing innovative capacity through the city deals programme. While this work continues I recognise that just as we emerge from the Covid crisis, we are facing once in a generation inflationary pressures driven by global factors. I meet regularly with businesses of all sizes across sectors to understand the pressures they are facing in that regard. That is why in recent weeks and months I have taken every opportunity to engage with UK Government to highlight these challenges – and I continue to work with it to ensure the additional support measures, such as the non-domestic Energy Price Bill Scheme, reach Northern Ireland businesses as soon as possible.  As the Finance Minister acknowledged recently in relation to the scale of the pressures facing businesses and households currently, the Northern Ireland Executive simply does not have the levers or financial firepower to deliver the type of immediate help at the scale that is required, whether the Executive is fully functioning or not.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether children who are deemed eligible for free school meals, but who have experienced delays in the processing of their applications, will have this backdated to the start of the school year.
answer_text: In recognising the importance of pupils’ Free School Meal and Uniform Grant entitlement being confirmed before the start of the academic year, the Education Authority (EA) has undertaken extensive communications explaining to families that in order to ensure they were able to access these supports from the start of the academic year, they should apply before 31 July. This was communicated via social media, the EA website and through letters issued to applicants who received support last year encouraging them to apply again for the current school year.  While many parents did apply early enough to ensure entitlement was in place for the start of the academic year, unfortunately this was not the case for all applications. The EA continues to make every effort to process applications as quickly as possible and has consistently met its target of processing 95% of free school meals and uniform grant applications within four weeks. As at 14 October, the EA was processing applications received on 16th September.In order to support parents in their applications, the EA provides extensive guidance materials and technical support. When an application is submitted without the appropriate documentation, all such applicants are sent a tailored email response explaining the correct documentation that they are required to provide. It is necessary to verify applicants’ eligibility in order to ensure that public funds are used correctly.Free school meals are a benefit in kind and there is therefore no facility available for providing backdated payments.

Senior officials from the Department are monitoring.Securing appropriate provision for children with statements of SEN remains a key priority for me and is being taken forward by the EA, supported by the Department and key partners, to ensure children are placed appropriately and in line with their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department will extend the Healthy Happy Minds funding post June 2022.
answer_text: Healthy Happy Minds, a pilot counselling and therapeutic support programme, has been introduced in all primary schools as one of a range measures supporting the implementation of the ‘The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ which was published in February 2021. The pilot was initially due to conclude at the end of March 2022 but was extended until June 2022.School Principals and Setting Leaders were advised on 6 July 2022 that I have allocated £2.25million to continue the Healthy Happy Minds programme until the end of December 2022.An independent evaluation, anticipated by Autumn 2022, will provide an opportunity for supporting evidence to be collated including the views of all stakeholders and will inform the establishment of a permanent service subject to Ministerial approval and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to (i) outline her Department's plans to address the need for additional accommodation at Rathore School, Newry; and (ii) provide an update on what stage these plans are at.
answer_text: The Education Authority has submitted a Business Case to my Department for approval for the provision of temporary SEN accommodation at St Columban’s Primary School, Kilkeel to facilitate pupils from Rathore from September 2022.The Business Case to provide a permanent solution for the additional accommodation required for Rathore School has also been completed and is currently with the Economists for review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many additional places at Rathore School, Newry will be provided for (i) pre-school; (ii) primary; and (iii) post-primary children, in plans for additional accommodation at the school.
answer_text: A number of potential long-term options are being explored by the Education Authority with a view to meeting the projected demand for special school places in Newry City and the surrounding area.  It is anticipated that an additional 18 classrooms across all key stages may be required to meet the future needs of the area to accommodate various Key Stages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education the current waiting times for children applying for (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary places in Rathore School, Newry.
answer_text: The Statutory Assessment process, as prescribed in legislation, manages each child’s placement on a case by case basis, which ensures they receive a suitable placement that will appropriately support and cater to their individual needs.  Children are informed once their school placement has been finalised, rather than on a fixed date. The placement of children for whom the Education Authority (EA) already maintains a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN), is managed through the Statutory Annual Review Process. The majority of those children who have been subject to the annual review of their statement of SEN, will have their placements confirmed between April and the end of June in any year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children are currently on the waiting list for Rathore School, Newry for September 2022 for (i) pre-school; (ii) primary; and (iii) post-primary school places.
answer_text: At 15 June 2022, the number of children currently awaiting confirmation of a placement for September 2022 was:(i)Pre School17(ii)Primary18(iii)Post Primary2

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has proposals to develop additional (i) day centre; and (ii) respite facilities, for (a) children; and (b) adults with learning disabilities in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) The Southern Health and Social Care Trust do not operate any day centres for Children with Disabilities (CWD) as they attend School.(ii) The CWD service provides short breaks via two residential facilities. Since onset of the pandemic the Southern HSC Trust increased capacity of each unit by increasing nights opened in one facility from 5 nights per week to 7 nights per week and increasing opening the 2nd facility from 4 nights per week to 6 nights per week. This increased capacity is retained and will remain so on a recurrent basis. There are no plans at present to develop an additional facility for short breaks provision within the Southern Trust.In addition to residential facilities, the CWD service has 21 Short Breaks fostering Households available providing short breaks to children with Disabilities. The Trust is committed to further expanding Short Breaks fostering and continues to actively promote recruitment of suitable carers.(i) The Southern Trust continues to work in partnership with existing and new providers to maintain existing day care places and increase the day care provision across the Southern Trust area. The Trust is currently working towards the opening of a new day care facility in the Newry area. This facility will provide day care for up to 25 service users with a Learning Disability. The Trust is aiming to have this service operational from July 2022.  The Southern Trust is also developing bespoke day care options for those individuals assessed as needing day care where existing services are mainly group based care,  do not always meet their assessed needs. Therefore additional onsite bespoke accommodation is being considered for 3 centres across the trust to accommodate individuals. This in turn will create additional space within the existing centres.(ii) The SHSCT has recently commissioned 4 additional residential short break / respite beds with a new provider in the Armagh area. In addition, 3 Nursing short break / respite beds have been commissioned with a provider in the Warrenpoint area. These nursing short break beds will be available in the coming weeks. The SHSCT continues to work with existing and new respite care providers to maintain existing contracted respite beds and to commission new beds to meet the needs of the Learning Disability population and their families

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his plans for the full restoration of day centre and respite services.
answer_text: In February 2022, the Chief Social Work Officer wrote to Trusts to outline my decision to remobilise respite services.  To date, significant efforts have been made by Trusts to implement the Public Health Agency Pathway to manage risk, while increasing service provision on a phased basis, throughout a period of high community prevalence of Covid-19. The latest position reported by Trusts shows that the majority have increased availability in adult day care.  However, this has not been reflected in the volume of attendances as a number of service users have been unable to attend due to health protection guidance that outlines that close contacts should not attend a Health & Social Care setting for a period of 10 days. Short Breaks provision across statutory health & social care settings has significantly increased.  However, overall capacity has reduced as a number of respite beds available in care homes have been withdrawn by independent sector providers. Transport continues to be provided on a reduced basis as there is limited scope to increase capacity while adhering to 1m physical distancing guidelines, aligned to recommendations by the World Health Organisation.While it is clear that the Pathway has been an enabler to remobilise services, there remains further adjustments to public health guidance required before services can be fully restore to pre-pandemic levels.  Therefore, my officials have written to the PHA asking them to commence an urgent formal review of the Pathway and associated health protection guidelines. Aligned to this process, the Department has requested performance and capacity data across all Trusts, which will be published on the Department’s website in June 2022.  I have clearly outlined that this data will be collected and published on a regular basis to monitor remobilisation against rebuild timeframes. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing a periodic communication to all MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts. It is regrettable that services have yet to be fully restored to pre-pandemic capacity and I sincerely apologise to those families that continue to receive a partial service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the costings for each proposed phase of the electricity upgrade for Daisy Hill Hospital, Newry.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust submitted a business case to upgrade the electrical network at Daisy Hill Hospital in May 2021, however due to budget constraints this is deemed unaffordable.The Trust is currently working on a revised business case which will propose a phased approach to the work required. The breakdown of costs for the phased approach are still being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the minor works scheme application for St Joseph’s High School, Newry.
answer_text: There are currently three active minor works applications for St Joseph’s High School:Health and Safety works to slope and path - this is currently with the Education Authority (EA) who are preparing a Feasibility Report and Business Case; Access Control – this is also with the EA for preparation of a Feasibility Report and Business Case.  My Department is currently awaiting additional information from the school to inform the Feasibility Report; and Electrical Works – this scheme is being progressed via Construction and Procurement Delivery, it is at the Detailed Design stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people waiting for (i) a new care package; and (ii) an increased care package, in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: This information was not held centrally so the information was requested from the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT). The SHSCT advised that on 06 June 2022 the SHSCT had 496 clients awaiting a Domiciliary Care Package.401 requests are for new care packages; and95 requests are for part packages. Of the 95 part packages, 13 are requests for an increased care package.Of the 496 clients, 18 clients are residing in Nursing/Residential facility waiting a new care package.They also advised that on 09 June 2022 there were 28 patients awaiting Residential and Nursing care packages.
